coredata.dc:title: Machine learning assisted paper-based fluorescent sensor array with metal-doped multicolor carbon quantum dots for identification and inactivation of bacteria
coredata.prism:copyright: © 2025 Elsevier B.V. All rights are reserved, including those for text and data mining, AI training, and similar technologies.
coredata.prism:publisher: Elsevier B.V. All rights are reserved, including those for text and data mining, AI training, and similar technologies.
coredata.dc:creator: Zhu, Liang
Mei, Lianghui
Xuan, Yan
Wang, Fangbin
coredata.dc:description: Bacterial infection is a thorny threat in a variety of fields, including medicine, environment, food, and agriculture. A multifunctional platform that meets the demands of both bacterial identification and real-time inactivation is urgently needed. This work introduces a novel paper-based fluorescence sensor array. It incorporates three types of metal-doped multicolor carbon quantum dots (CQDs)—Ag-CQDs, Cu-CQDs, and Zn-CQDs—deposited directly onto filter paper using an inkjet printer. The presence of various bacteria reduces the fluorescence of the CQDs. These changes are detected with a smartphone and analyzed through a machine learning algorithm, facilitating accurate identification of the bacteria. The results reveal the platform's capacity to recognize five kinds of bacteria across a wide concentration range from 1.0 × 103 to 1.0 × 107 CFU/mL, underlining its versatility in detecting different levels of bacteria. The platform also exhibits antibacterial capacity, reaching a better antibacterial effect within 30 min. This platform is not only cost-effective and highly integrated but also provides a concept for designing multifunctional biosensors for on-site bacterial detection and antibacterial applications across various fields.
coredata.dcterms:subject: Bacterial identification
Antibacterial application
Sensor array
CQDs
Machine learning

Characterization of materials and sensing mechanism
Sensor array fluorescence reactions to bacteria
Recognition of unknown and real samples

ce:caption.ce:simple-para: TEM images of (A) Ag-CQDs, (B) Cu-CQDs and (C) Zn-CQDs, with an inset displaying the diameter distribution of CQDs. (D) XPS and (E) FTIR spectra of CQDs.

Fluorescence emission spectra of (A) Ag-CQDs, (B) Cu-CQDs, and (C) Zn-CQDs in different excitation wavelengths. Fluorescence spectra of (D) Ag-CQDs, (E) Cu-CQDs and (F) Zn-CQDs after exposure to varying concentrations of E. coli.

Paper-based sensor array (A) before and (B) after the introduction of bacteria. (C) Variation in relative signal intensity of the sensor array responding to five bacterial types at a concentration of 1.0 × 103 CFU/mL. (D) Efficiency in identifying individual unknown samples. (E) Confusion matrix illustrating the KNN classification results for a single unknown sample. (F) Parallel coordinate plot displaying the three primary factors (Factor 1, Factor 2, Factor 3) of the unknown individual sample.

(A) Representation of five bacterial categories in feature space utilizing the KNN algorithm. (B) Canonical score visualization for identifying individual bacterial samples. (C) Dendrogram originated from KNN algorithm by using average linkage method of the five types of bacteria. (D) Heatmap plot originated from KNN algorithm of the five types of bacteria.

(A) Radar diagram illustrating E. coli across varying concentrations. (B) Canonical score graph utilized for identifying E. coli at different levels. (C) Plot displaying Factor 1 in relation to E. coli concentration. Error bars indicate the standard deviation from three replicates. (D) Canonical score diagram for recognizing unknown individual bacterial samples. (E) Canonical score graph employed to identify unidentified bacterial samples in tap water.

(A) Photographs of E. coli and S. aureus plates after exposure to Ag-CQDs, Cu-CQDs, and Zn-CQDs, respectively. (B) Graph illustrating the antibacterial effectiveness of CQDs as determined by the plate count method. (C) SEM images showing morphological alterations in E. coli and S. aureus following CQD treatment. (D) Calcein-AM/PI stained images of E. coli and S. aureus. Data are presented as mean ± SD (n = 3). Statistical evaluations were conducted using one-way ANOVA with Tukey's post hoc test. ∗∗∗p < 0.001.
Diagram and explanation of the paper-based array's design and operation for bacterial identification and inactivation.

head.ce:title: Machine learning assisted paper-based fluorescent sensor array with metal-doped multicolor carbon quantum dots for identification and inactivation of bacteria

ce:affiliation.ce:label: a
ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: School of Food and Nutrition, Anhui Agricultural University, Hefei, 230036, China
ce:affiliation.ce:source-text: School of Food and Nutrition, Anhui Agricultural University, Hefei, 230036, China

ce:affiliation.ce:label: b
ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: School of Food and Biological Engineering, Hefei University of Technology, Hefei, 230009, China

sa:affiliation.sa:organization: School of Food and Biological Engineering

ce:affiliation.ce:source-text: School of Food and Biological Engineering, Hefei University of Technology, Hefei, 230009, China

ce:affiliation.ce:label: c
ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: School of Life Science, Anhui Medical University, Hefei, 230032, China
ce:affiliation.ce:source-text: School of Life Science, Anhui Medical University, Hefei, 230032, China

ce:abstract-sec.ce:simple-para: Bacterial infection is a thorny threat in a variety of fields, including medicine, environment, food, and agriculture. A multifunctional platform that meets the demands of both bacterial identification and real-time inactivation is urgently needed. This work introduces a novel paper-based fluorescence sensor array. It incorporates three types of metal-doped multicolor carbon quantum dots (CQDs)—Ag-CQDs, Cu-CQDs, and Zn-CQDs—deposited directly onto filter paper using an inkjet printer. The presence of various bacteria reduces the fluorescence of the CQDs. These changes are detected with a smartphone and analyzed through a machine learning algorithm, facilitating accurate identification of the bacteria. The results reveal the platform's capacity to recognize five kinds of bacteria across a wide concentration range from 1.0 × 103 to 1.0 × 107 CFU/mL, underlining its versatility in detecting different levels of bacteria. The platform also exhibits antibacterial capacity, reaching a better antibacterial effect within 30 min. This platform is not only cost-effective and highly integrated but also provides a concept for designing multifunctional biosensors for on-site bacterial detection and antibacterial applications across various fields.

ce:data-availability.ce:para: Data will be made available on request.

ce:section.ce:para: The rising threat of bacterial infections presents significant challenges across various sectors including healthcare, food safety, and environmental monitoring. These infections not only lead to severe health issues but also impose substantial economic burdens. Traditional methods for bacterial detection, such as bacterial culture [1], micro-observation of bacteria [2] and enzyme-linked immunosorbent assays (ELISA), often fall short due to their complexity, time-consuming processes, and the need for specialized equipment [3]. Moreover, these methods typically lack the capability to simultaneously identify and inactivate bacteria, which is crucial for preventing the spread of infections and ensuring public safety.

Sensor arrays have emerged as a powerful analytical tool for bacterial identification due to their high-throughput detection capability [4–6]. These arrays work by generating unique response patterns, for different bacterial species, which can be analyzed using machine learning algorithms [7,8]. By leveraging the diverse affinities of sensing materials to various bacterial strains, sensor arrays can provide rapid and accurate identification without the need for specific recognition receptors preparation [9]. Fluorescent sensor arrays are attracting significant interest for bacterial identification [10]. several investigations have shown their efficacy in bacterial identification. For example, a sensor array utilizing antibiotic-stabilized metal nanoclusters allows for the simultaneous detection of multiple bacterial species [11]. A hybrid nanomaterial sensor array combining gold nanoparticles and transition-metal carbides (MXenes) was employed to classify bacteria [12]. Graphene oxide and tungsten disulfide nanomaterials are used as fluorescence quenchers, combined with fluorescence-labeled ssDNA, to form a sensing array for pathogenic microorganism identification [13]. Furthermore, the array sensor was used to establish a unique chemical reaction "fingerprint" database for the mechanisms of action. By combining it with a neural network-based genetic algorithm, the differentiation of antibiotics was achieved [14].

Although the aforementioned sensor arrays for identifying bacteria have some advantages, they also exhibit several limitations. For instance, the requirement for bulky fluorescence measurement equipment results in poor portability. The preparation of these sensor arrays is often complicated and time-consuming, making them less conducive to long-term storage and practical use in field settings. Furthermore, most of these sensor arrays focus solely on bacterial identification and do not incorporate bacterial elimination, which is essential for preventing bacterial contamination and infection during the detection process [15]. Thus,the need for a portable multifunctional platform that combines both identification and elimination of bacteria is thus critical to address these shortcomings.

In this study, leveraging the bactericidal mechanisms of heavy metal ions, we prepared a multifunctional paper-based fluorescent sensor array capable of simultaneously distinguishing and inactivating bacteria. Metal-doped multicolor carbon quantum dots (Ag-CQDs, Cu-CQDs, and Zn-CQDs) serve as sensing units. An inkjet printer directly patterns these CQDs onto paper substrates, simplifying fabrication and integrating the sensor array. Different types of bacteria exhibit varying affinities towards the platform, resulting in different degrees of fluorescence quenching. The fluorescence signals from the platform are read using a smartphone, enabling rapid bacterial identification with the aid of a machine learning algorithm (Scheme 1 ). Additionally, the platform demonstrates strong antibacterial activity, effectively inactivating bacteria post-detection without the need for additional inactivation treatments. It provides a new perspective for bacterial detection and antibacterial applications in fields such as medicine, food, agriculture, and the environment.

Ethyl alcohol, alkyl ketene dimer (AKD), glycol, Triton X-100, copper chloride, silver nitrate, and zinc chloride were obtained from Aladdin Reagent Co., Ltd. l-tryptophan, citric acid, 4-aminophenol, ethylenediamine, o-phenylenediamine and phosphate-buffered saline (PBS) were obtained from Macklin Reagent Co., Ltd. Filter paper was acquired from Hangzhou Double Circle Filter Paper Co., Ltd.

The optical density (OD) of bacterial cultures was measured using an Eppendorf BioSpectrometer basic. Fluorescence spectra of the CQDs were obtained with an Agilent Cary Eclipse fluorescence spectrophotometer. The transmission electron microscopy (TEM, JEM-2100F) was used to characterize CQDs morphology. X-ray photoelectron spectroscopy (XPS) was performed using an ESCALab220-Xi instrument. Functional groups and chemical bonds of the nanoparticles were identified by Fourier transform infrared spectroscopy (FTIR, Nicolet iS50). An array pattern was printed onto paper using an HP Desk Jet 1112 inkjet printer.

Three types of metal-doped multicolored CQDs were prepared via a hydrothermal method [16–18]. For Cu-CQDs, 0.17 g l-tryptophan, 0.19 g citric acid, and 0.28 g copper chloride were dissolved in 30 mL H2O, then transferred to a 50 mL Teflon-lined autoclave and heated at 180 °C for 3 h. The resultant mixture was centrifuged at 10,000 rpm for 10 min, and dialyzed against double distilled water using a 500 Da MWCO membrane for 48 h. For Ag-CQDs, 0.15 g 4-aminobenzene, 2 mL ethylenediamine, and 0.3 g silver nitrate were dissolved in 20 mL water. The solution was processed under the same hydrothermal conditions at 180 °C but for 12 h, followed by same centrifugation and dialysis process. For Zn-CQDs, 0.23 g o-phenylenediamine and 0.28 g zinc chloride were dissolved in 25 mL ethanol. The solution was processed under the same hydrothermal conditions at 180 °C but for 12 h, followed by the same centrifugation and dialysis process.

Three fluorescent inks were formulated by combining 10 mg/mL Cu-CQDs, 8 mg/mL Ag-CQDs, or 12 mg/mL Zn-CQDs with Triton X-100 (surfactant) and ethylene glycol (viscosity modifier). A hydrophobic ink was developed by mixing 15 mg/mL of the sizing agent AKD with Sudan Red dye, which is primarily used to distinguish between the hydrophilic and hydrophobic areas of the sensor array.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Construction of the paper-based sensor array and visual detection device
ce:section.ce:para: The sensor array pattern was designed as a 3 × 5 matrix of circles, each with a radius of 3 mm. Patterns were printed onto paper using an inkjet printer with hydrophobic ink in the black cartridge and fluorescent ink in the color cartridge. After printing, the paper was baked at 100 °C for 5 min. A visual detection device was assembled using a smartphone and a dark box. The dark box, measuring 8 cm × 6 cm × 8 cm were fabricated by 3D printing with black resin material (Fig. S1). Inside the dark box, two 365 nm UV LED beads and a UV filter were installed. The RGB values of the images were obtained using a smartphone.

Escherichia coli (E. coli, ATCC25312), Salmonella typhimurium (S. typhimurium, ATCC14028), Pseudomonas aeruginosa (P. aeruginosa, ATCC 15442), Listeria monocytogenes (L. monocytogenes, ATCC 19115) and Staphylococcus aureus (S. aureus, ATCC 29213) were used in this research. For each of the five bacterial species cultured on solid Luria-Bertani (LB) medium, a single colony was selected and washed twice with 3 mL of phosphate-buffered saline (PBS). The bacterial suspensions were then measured using a spectrophotometer to determine their OD. Based on a pre-established CFU–OD600 standard curve, the suspensions were diluted with PBS to concentrations of 1.0 × 103–1.0 × 107 CFU/mL for subsequent use.

Bacterial samples at various concentrations were applied to the sensor array and incubated for 10 min. After incubation, the sensor array was analyzed using a detection device, and the RGB values were captured with the Color Grab app. To calculate the relative change in signal intensity (ΔRGB), the equation ΔRGB = (R0 – R)/R0+(G0 – G)/G0+(B0 – B)/B0 was used. In this formula, R0, G0, and B0 represent the red, green, and blue values from images without bacteria, while R, G, and B are the corresponding values with bacteria present. To generate a data matrix for identification (three CQDs × five bacteria × ten repetitions), the sensor array experiment was repeated ten times for each of the five bacterial species at different concentrations. The resulting data were analyzed using machine learning algorithms, including k-nearest neighbors (KNN), linear discriminant analysis (LDA), support vector machines (SVM), naive Bayes classifiers, and decision trees. Detailed descriptions of these algorithms are provided in the Supporting Information.

Antibacterial assays were conducted using Escherichia coli and Staphylococcus aureus. Bacterial suspensions were washed and resuspended in PBS for all experiments. The samples were assigned to four groups at random: control, Ag-CQDs, Cu-CQDs, and Zn-CQDs. Bacterial samples incubated in blank buffer formed the control group, providing a baseline for comparison. The bactericidal efficacy of the four treatments against the tested strains was determined using agar plate enumeration.

To enhance the visualization and comparison of the bactericidal effects of the three CQDs, calcein-AM and propidium iodide (PI) staining was employed. Following a 3-h incubation of the CQD samples with bacterial suspensions, the samples were rinsed with PBS. Calcein-AM and PI were then added at concentrations of 1.5 μL/mL each, and the samples were incubated in the dark at 37 °C for 45 min. Finally, Bacterial viability was assessed.

To assess the performance of the machine learning algorithms for bacterial identification, several standard metrics were utilized. These included accuracy, positive predictive value (PPV), true positive rate (TPR), F1-score, parallel coordinate plots, and confusion matrices. The following equations were employed to calculate these metrics:
(1)
Accuracy = TP + TN /
(
TP + TN + TP + FP
)
(2)
PPV = TP /
(
TP + FP
)
(3)
TPR = TP /
(
TP + FN
)
(4)
F 1 − score = 2 × PPV × TPR /
(
PPV + TPR
)
In these equations, TP represents true positives, FP represents false positives, TN represents true negatives, and FN represents false negatives.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Characterization of materials and sensing mechanism
ce:section.ce:para: Fig. 1 A–C displays the SEM images of Ag-CQDs, Cu-CQDs, and Zn-CQDs, respectively. Three types of CQDs exhibited a consistent spherical morphology, with average diameters of 2.20 ± 0.52 nm, 2.40 ± 0.55 nm, and 2.84 ± 0.57 nm. High-resolution TEM images (Fig. S2) demonstrate the presence of graphitic carbon diffraction planes in each type of CQDs, displaying interlayer lattice spacings ranging from 0.1 to 0.3 nm. XPS spectra provide detailed binding energy profiles for the CQDs. XPS spectra presented for Ag-doped, Cu-doped, and Zn-doped carbon quantum dots (CQDs) reveal distinct elemental signatures (Fig. 1D). Notable peaks include Ag 3d in Ag-CQDs, Cu 2p in Cu-CQDs, and Zn 2p in Zn-CQDs, demonstrating the successful incorporation of these metals. All CQDs also exhibit peaks for C 1s, N 1s and O 1s, indicating the presence of carbon, nitrogen and oxygen. For Ag-CQDs, the Ag 3d peaks confirm the presence of silver, as seen in the detailed spectrum with peaks at 374.36 eV and 368.33 eV for Ag 3d3/2 and Ag 3d5/2, respectively (Fig. S3A) [19]. In Cu-CQDs spectrum, the distinct Cu 2p peaks confirm the presence of copper, with the high-resolution spectrum showing peaks at 954.2 eV and 934.4 eV for Cu 2p1/2 and Cu 2p3/2, respectively (Fig. S3B) [20]. For Zn-CQDs, the Zn 2p peaks confirm the incorporation of zinc, with the high-resolution spectrum showing peaks at 1045.46 eV and 1022.49 eV for Zn 2p1/2 and Zn 2p3/2, respectively (Fig. S3C) [21]. The consistent presence at 531.56 eV for O 1s, 399.46 eV for N 1s, and 284.63 eV for C 1s in the spectra of the three types of CQDs indicate the presence of oxygen, nitrogen, and carbon [22]. FTIR was employed to thoroughly investigate the functional groups present in the CQDs. As depicted in Fig. 1E, the absorption band around 3448.65 cm−1 corresponds to the stretching vibrations of N/O–H groups. Additionally, the peaks at 1706.53 cm−1 and 1624.21 cm−1 are indicative of the stretching vibrations of CO and CN bonds, respectively. Furthermore, the absorption band at 1396.48 cm−1 is associated with the CC structure [23]. These findings suggest that the surfaces of the three types of carbon quantum dots are rich in amino and carboxyl groups, which facilitate chelation reactions with metals, thereby promoting metal doping and surface passivation processes. The absorption band at 662.83 cm−1 in Ag-CQDs corresponds to the Ag–N group [19]. In contrast, Cu-CQDs display a peak at 945.17 cm−1, which is associated with the N–Cu–N group [20]. The peaks at 764.07 cm−1 and 701.52 cm−1 in Zn-CQDs can be attributed to the Zn–O group [21].

Fig. 2 A–C displays the fluorescence emission spectra for Ag-doped, Cu-doped, and Zn-doped CQDs measured across excitation wavelengths ranging from 300 nm to 420 nm. Notably, the emission bands exhibit minimal shifts in response to changes of the excitation wavelength. In contrast, the fluorescence intensities at 509 nm, 443 nm, and 554 nm, corresponding to Ag-CQDs, Cu-CQDs, and Zn-CQDs, respectively, exhibit a noteworthy pattern. They have maximum emission fluorescence intensity excited by 400 nm, 300 nm, and 400 nm excitation wavelength, respectively. The findings indicate that CQDs maintain consistent emission wavelengths despite changes in excitation wavelength, whereas fluorescence intensity varies with the excitation conditions.

The antibacterial properties of metal ions were leveraged to develop a sensor array for the identification and elimination of bacteria. The bacterial surface, containing negatively charged substances such as phosphate and carboxyl groups, exhibits electronegativity in physiological environments. By doping with metal ions such as Ag, Cu, Zn, the CQDs are surface-modified with positive charges, which leads to strong electrostatic adsorption with bacteria (Fig. S4). This charge-driven effect promotes the accumulation and aggregation of CQDs on the bacterial surface, thereby triggering the aggregation-caused quenching effect. This phenomenon arises from the shortened distance between CQDs in the aggregated state, leading to energy/electron transfer, which in turn causes non-radiative decay and decreased fluorescence intensity. As the bacterial concentration increases, the aggregation of CQDs intensifies, and the fluorescence quenching efficiency increases in a concentration-dependent manner, providing the basis for quantitative bacterial detection. It is important to note that different metal doping leads to variations in the surface charge density distribution of CQDs, which directly affects the strength of the interaction between the CQDs and bacteria. Meanwhile, due to differences in the cell membrane composition of different bacterial strains, their surface electronegativity varies, resulting in different binding affinities for CQDs. When three metal-doped CQDs (Ag-CQDs, Cu-CQDs, Zn-CQDs) interact with five bacterial species, a fluorescence quenching characteristic matrix can be formed. The core of this sensor array lies in constructing a multi-dimensional response pattern: the interaction between each bacterium and metal-doped CQDs induces unique fluorescence quenching characteristics, enabling precise bacterial identification through machine learning algorithms.

Furthermore, Ag, Cu, and Zn, as broad-spectrum antibacterial metal elements, cooperate in bactericidal activity when their doped CQDs bind to bacteria by disrupting cell membrane integrity, inducing proZtein denaturation, binding DNA to inhibit replication, and generating reactive oxygen species [24]. The metal ions continuously accumulate on the bacterial surface, effectively inhibiting bacterial metabolism and proliferation over a long period, allowing the sensor to achieve simultaneous detection and bacterial killing without the need for additional inactivation treatments.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Sensor array fluorescence reactions to bacteria
ce:section.ce:para: The sensor array detects bacteria by causing metal-doped CQDs to accumulate on their surfaces, which leads to the quenching of fluorescence. We used five bacteria (E. coli, P. aeruginosa, S. aureus, S. typhimurium, and L. monocytogenes) as models. As depicted in Fig. 2D–F, increasing concentrations of E. coli result in a reduction of the sensor array's fluorescence intensity. The different affinities of E. coli to the three metal-doped CQDs cause varied fluorescence quenching effects in the array's sensing units. Similar results were observed for other bacteria (Figs. S5–S8).

The results demonstrate that variations in bacterial size, shape, and surface negative charge groups cause distinct interactions with metal-doped CQDs, leading to different levels of fluorescence quenching. This variability enables the development of a sensor array capable of differentiating bacteria by utilizing the unique cross-responses between various metal-doped CQDs and the bacterial cells.

To develop a bacterial detection system that is swift, effective, and economical, an innovative platform was designed by integrating three primary components (Scheme 1). Initially, the bacterial solution is deposited onto the array. Subsequently, the array is inserted into the detection enclosure to facilitate fluorescence excitation. Finally, a smartphone records the emitted fluorescence image through the enclosure's port. The viscosity and surface tension of the ink are critical parameters in inkjet printing. These properties govern the release of ink droplets and their deposition onto the filter paper, ultimately influencing the array's sensing performance.

To achieve this, Triton X-100 and ethylene glycol were incorporated to modify the ink's surface tension and viscosity, respectively. Optimal proportions were established by comparing with commercial inks. As shown in Table 1 , Triton X-100 and ethylene glycol were added at concentrations of 5 % and 10 %, respectively. Fig. S9 illustrates the sensor array printed on filter paper using the final ink formulation. Notably, this paper-based sensor array can be fabricated in merely 20 s.

To evaluate the fluorescent sensor array's ability to distinguish different bacteria, five bacterial species were applied at a concentration of 1.0 × 103 CFU/mL. Fig. 3 A–B illustrates the fluorescence color changes observed in the sensor array after interaction with these bacteria. By calculating the changes in the fluorescence color values (ΔRGB), it was demonstrated that each type of bacteria elicited a distinct response from the sensor array (Fig. 3C). The varying interactions between bacteria and metal-doped CQDs arise from differences in bacterial size, and negatively charged surface groups. Following this, pattern recognition methods incorporating various machine learning algorithms were employed to analyze the ΔRGB values. In the training stage, the dataset was randomly partitioned into two subsets: 70 % allocated for model development and the remaining 30 % designated for evaluation. Different machine learning models were evaluated for each dataset based on their effectiveness. All available classification algorithms in MATLAB were utilized for bacterial discrimination, without presuming any algorithm's superiority.

Several machine learning techniques were assessed, including Nearest Neighbors (KNN), Naive Bayes, Decision Trees, Linear Discriminant Analysis (LDA), and Support Vector Machines (SVM). Four core indicators were used for performance evaluation to determine sensitivity: positive predictive value (PPV), true positive rate (TPR), F1-score, and prediction accuracy. As depicted in Fig. 3D, the sensor array in this study has a good classification effect on the five bacteria, and the four core indicators have reached 100 %.

The KNN algorithm was selected for subsequent analysis owing to its simplicity, ease of understanding and implementation, and high accuracy. Furthermore, KNN is particularly effective for multi-class classification tasks, providing advantages over the other four evaluated algorithms. Fig. 3E presents the confusion matrix, clearly indicating that the machine learning algorithm achieved perfect scores in accuracy, TPR, and PPV. This exceptional performance validates the effectiveness of the employed machine learning methods for bacterial identification. A parallel coordinate plot was created (Fig. 3F) to thoroughly depict the interactions among the three primary factors determined by the machine learning algorithm. This visual tool effectively emphasizes intricate patterns and connections within the multivariate dataset, offering significant insights into the complex relationships among the chosen factors.

To further confirm the sensor array's ability to distinguish between bacteria using the KNN algorithm, a 3D feature space plot was generated with three canonical factors derived from a subset of individual bacterial samples (Fig. 4 A). 50 samples (5 types of bacteria ✕ 10 repetitions) can be well divided into five groups, with no overlap between groups. Additionally, the top two most important discrimination factors were chosen to validate sample differentiation (Fig. 4B). All individual bacterial samples were successfully identified, highlighting the sensor array's high accuracy in bacterial discrimination when assisted by the machine learning algorithm.

Euclidean distance calculations were employed to distinguish all five bacterial species. Fig. 4C presents the dendrogram constructed using hierarchical cluster analysis, effectively separating five unique clusters, each representing a distinct bacterial species. Additionally, a heatmap was generated to provide further support for the specific classification of the five bacterial types. The heatmap illustrates that introducing bacteria led to unique fluorescence response patterns (Fig. 4D). These results further underscore the high feasibility of using ML algorithm in conjunction with the sensor array for bacterial identification.

The presence of diverse bacteria in natural environments presents a challenge and is crucial for differentiating bacterial mixtures. To evaluate the sensor array's ability to identify combined bacterial populations, E. coli and S. aureus were mixed at a concentration of 1.0 × 103 CFU/mL. Mixtures in varying proportions were analyzed using the developed sensor array. A confusion matrix was employed for this analysis (Fig. S10A). Machine learning algorithm evaluation revealed that the array could accurately distinguish bacterial mixtures, as shown in Fig. S10B. These results demonstrate the capability of the sensor array for distinguish bacterial mixtures.

To determine the sensor array's performance, the differentiation of bacteria at various concentrations was investigated. Radar plots generated from the ΔRGB values of the sensor array in response to different concentrations of E. coli (Fig. 5 A and S11) display distinct patterns corresponding to each concentration, indicating that the sensor array provides unique chromatic signatures and demonstrates strong discrimination ability for bacterial concentrations. The recorded ΔRGB changes for different bacterial concentrations were further analyzed using 2D typical score plots (Fig. 5B and S12), which clearly show distinct separation among clusters of varying concentrations. The most influential factor, Factor 1, accounted for more than 80 % of the variance in the score plots [25]. Thus, using Factor 1 as a correlational function for the five bacterial concentrations is justified. Factor 1 is strongly linearly correlated with logarithm of bacterial concentrations, as illustrated in Fig. 5C and S13. The limit of detection (LOD) was calculated by using the formula: LOD = 3σ/S, where σ represents the standard deviation of blank measurements (n = 10), and S is the slope of the calibration curve. Table S1 displays the sensor array's LOD for five bacterial species. By comparing Factor 1 values from unknown samples with the calibration curve, the concentration of bacteria in the sample can be accurately estimated.

Compared to other sensor arrays reported in the literature, this study's platform demonstrates higher sensitivity in differentiating low bacterial concentrations (Table S2). Additionally, by using paper-based material for the sensor array, the platform offers advantages such as portability and ease of use. By dropping the sample solution onto the paper substrate and observing changes in fluorescence images under UV light, bacterial presence can be qualitatively detected. Combined with smartphone camera operation, this platform provides an attractive alternative for on-site bacterial detection, especially in resource-limited environments.

When compared with other reported methods, the developed platform demonstrated strong recognition capability (Table S3). In summary, these findings reveal that sensor array is capable of both identifying and quantifying bacterial concentrations concurrently.

This study also explores the platform's ability to resist interference. As shown in Fig. S14, even in the presence of interfering substances (Na+, Cl−, glucose, galactose, and arginine), the assay successfully differentiates between the five bacteria, highlighting the platform's exceptional sensitivity. Additionally, the results in Fig. S15 show that the sensing responses remained stable with no significant decrease after 15 days and exhibited excellent repeatability across 20 repetitive measurements. These findings confirm the platform's effectiveness in identifying bacteria in real samples.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Recognition of unknown and real samples
ce:section.ce:para: The platform's effectiveness was tested with unknown samples, including single bacterial species and binary combinations of the five bacteria, each maintained at a concentration of 1.0 × 103 CFU/mL. Fig. 5D–S16 and S17 demonstrate the accurate identification of 60 unknown samples, with detailed results presented in Table S3. To evaluate the device's suitability for practical applications, twelve unidentified bacterial samples were tested in tap water, each maintained at a concentration of 1.0 × 103 CFU/mL. All five bacterial species were accurately distinguished in water samples, as depicted in Fig. 5E and detailed in Table S4. These outcomes validate the platform's capability and consistency in recognizing the bacterial species within real sample environments.

The fluorescent sensor array composed of the three metal-doped CQDs demonstrated strong antibacterial properties. In this study, E. coli and S. aureus were chosen to evaluate the antibacterial capabilities of the sensor array. The plate counting technique was utilized to measure the sensor array's antibacterial efficacy against these bacterial strains. Images of bacterial colonies treated with the control, Ag-CQDs, Cu-CQDs, and Zn-CQDs for 30 min were captured (Fig. 6 A). Colony counts indicated that all three CQDs groups exhibited significant bactericidal effects (Fig. 6B). Specifically, the antibacterial efficacies of the Ag-CQDs, Cu-CQDs, and Zn-CQDs against E. coli were 99.99 %, 87.2 %, and 74.8 %, respectively, while against S. aureus, they were 99.92 %, 86.1 %, and 68.9 %, respectively. Further analysis using SEM revealed distinct morphological changes in the bacteria exposed to the CQDs (Fig. 6C). The untreated E. coli cells maintained their typical rod shape, and S. aureus cells retained their spherical form with smooth and intact cell walls. In contrast, bacteria treated with the CQDs showed significant morphological alterations, including membrane wrinkling and shrinkage in E. coli and a loss of the spherical shape in S. aureus. Additionally, live/dead staining with calcein-AM and propidium iodide (PI) supported these findings, as increased red fluorescence and diminished green fluorescence indicated extensive damage to bacterial cell membranes and reduced viability following CQDs treatment (Fig. 6D). In conclusion, the sensor array exhibited excellent antibacterial activity, effectively reducing bacterial viability through significant damage to cell membrane integrity.

In summary, a multifunctional paper-based fluorescent sensor array has been successfully developed for simultaneous bacteria identification and inactivation. The array's sensing units are composed of multicolor carbon quantum dots (CQDs) doped with metals (Ag-CQDs, Cu-CQDs, and Zn-CQDs), each showing different affinities for various types of bacteria, resulting in distinct levels of fluorescence quenching. This allows for the rapid identification of five bacteria types using machine learning algorithms. A portable paper-based sensor array was created via inkjet printing technology, exhibiting acceptable sensitivity and stability, capable of distinguishing five bacteria at concentrations as low as 1.0 × 103 CFU/mL and measures bacterial levels ranging from 1.0 × 103 to 1.0 × 107 CFU/mL. Notably, it was effectively used for differentiating between real and unknown samples. In terms of antibacterial application, the paper-based sensor array achieved a better antibacterial effect within 30 min without the need for additional inactivation processes. This platform is not only cost-effective and highly integrated but also offers new prospects for bacterial detection and antibacterial applications in fields such as medicine, food, agriculture, and the environment.

Liang Zhu: Writing – original draft, Methodology, Data curation, Conceptualization. Lianghui Mei: Methodology, Formal analysis, Data curation. Yan Xuan: Investigation, Formal analysis. Fangbin Wang: Writing – review & editing, Funding acquisition, Conceptualization.

This work was supported by the National Natural Science Foundation of China Grants (32471292), Anhui Provincial Natural Science Foundation Grant (2408085MC075) and the Fundamental Research Funds for the Central Universities (JZ2023HGTB0232).

ce:conflict-of-interest.ce:para: The authors declare that they have no known competing financial interests or personal relationships that could have appeared to influence the work reported in this paper.
